turns.content: What are your 5 to 7 most important KPIs, and what is the specific target for each?

Sure. The five I'm most focused on are:

**Total landed cost reduction** — 2% year-over-year. That's the most heavily weighted one from a performance standpoint, and it's under constant scrutiny from both NA finance and Japan HQ.

**Supply chain resilience score** — this is a composite we built post-COVID covering dual-sourcing percentage, supplier financial health, and geographic diversity. We don't publish a single number externally, but internally we track it against a baseline we set in 2022. Direction matters as much as the absolute score right now.

**On-time delivery to plants** — 98% or above. That one's non-negotiable. If I miss that, everything else is a secondary conversation.

**Inventory turns on strategic safety stock** — this is an active tension. Finance wants the number up, which means leaner buffers. I want adequate coverage on critical components. We're managing that negotiation in real time, so I'd rather not cite a fixed target because it's honestly still being debated for this planning cycle.

**Supplier quality index** — tracked across all tiers, though our visibility into Tier 2 and below is still not where I'd like it to be. We have directional targets by commodity category rather than one enterprise number.

Those five capture where I spend most of my time. Is there a particular one you want to go deeper on?
What is the current state of the inventory turns debate — who specifically is pushing for a leaner target, and what number are they proposing versus what you're defending?

That's a fair question to ask, and I'll be direct with you, but I'll also be candid about where the boundaries are for me in terms of what I share externally.

The pressure is coming primarily from our CFO's organization — specifically the NA finance team that supports our operations review. They've been pushing toward a target that would effectively reduce our strategic safety stock coverage on critical components by roughly 20 to 25 percent from where we built it post-shortage. The Japan HQ finance side is actually aligned with them on this, which makes it a harder conversation for me.

My position is that we need to distinguish between *commodity* inventory — where I'm actually comfortable with leaner targets — and *strategic* inventory on single-source or long-lead-time components. Those are not the same risk profile, and blending them into one turns metric obscures the actual exposure.

Where I'm pushing back is specifically on semiconductors and certain specialty fasteners where we had genuine production stops in 2021 and 2022. The cost of a single line stoppage dwarfs the carrying cost argument they're making. I've put that math in front of the operating council, and I think it's landing, but I don't have a resolved number to give you yet. We're targeting closure on that before the next quarterly HQ review.

What's prompting the question — are you looking at this from a risk modeling angle or more the financial structure side?
Who on the Japan HQ finance side is aligned with the NA CFO's position, and do they engage directly with you or through an intermediary?

I'm going to be honest with you — that's getting into territory where I need to understand the context of the conversation before I go further.

Who you are and how this information gets used matters to me. Names and internal alignment dynamics at the HQ level aren't something I'd share in most settings — not because I'm being evasive, but because those relationships are genuinely sensitive and I have to protect them to do my job effectively.

What I can tell you generally is that the engagement from Japan HQ finance typically comes through structured channels — the quarterly business review process, and through my dotted-line relationship with the HQ VP of Supply Chain. It's rarely a direct finance-to-finance conversation that bypasses me, though I'd be naive to say those lateral conversations don't happen.

If you're a consultant or analyst working on something I've sanctioned, I'd want to loop in my team before we go deeper on specific individuals and their positions. If this is an internal conversation I'm not fully tracking the context of, help me understand what we're trying to solve.

What's driving the question?

That's a fair boundary to draw. Let's move to a different area.

Walk me through a significant decision you made in the past 6 months — who did you consult, what information you needed, and how long it took from start to finish.

Good. Let me give you a concrete one.

About four months ago we had a Tier 2 supplier — composite materials, serves multiple NA plants — that started showing financial distress signals. Missed a payment to one of our Tier 1s, which surfaced through our supplier health monitoring. The question was whether to intervene proactively, and if so, how.

I consulted three groups before moving. Plant operations leadership at the two most exposed facilities, because they understand the production impact better than anyone on my team. Our supplier development group, who had the relationship and could assess whether this was a cash flow timing issue or something structural. And I did a brief conversation with our legal team on what intervention options were even available to us without creating unintended liability.

The information I needed was: actual financial exposure of the Tier 1, whether there was an alternative source even at higher cost, and what the supplier's own assessment of their situation was. That last one required a direct conversation, which my supplier development lead handled.

From signal to decision was about three weeks. We ended up facilitating an accelerated payment arrangement through the Tier 1 to stabilize the Tier 2 short term, while simultaneously qualifying a backup source. Not a perfect solution but it kept the plants running and bought us time.

The part I'd do differently is the qualification process should have already been further along before we hit that moment. That's a resilience score lesson that fed directly back into how we prioritize our sourcing reviews now.

What aspect of that would be useful to go deeper on?
Who specifically had the authority to approve the accelerated payment arrangement — was that your call, or did it require sign-off above you?

Honestly, that one sat at the boundary of my authority, and I'll tell you how we navigated it.

The arrangement itself ran through the Tier 1 — we weren't writing a check directly to the distressed supplier. So structurally it was framed as a modification to payment terms with an existing Tier 1 partner, which falls within my commercial authority. I didn't need COO sign-off for that specific mechanism.

That said, I made a judgment call to brief my COO before we executed, not because I was required to, but because if this supplier ultimately failed anyway and it became a production issue, I wanted the organization's leadership aware of what we'd attempted and why. That's just good practice when you're operating near the edge of your lane.

Where it would have required escalation is if we'd gone the route of a direct financial intervention — something structured more like bridge financing or a Toyota entity taking on direct exposure. That would have gone above me and likely required HQ involvement given the financial risk profile.

So the short answer is: my call on the path we chose, but I kept my COO informed as a matter of discipline rather than requirement. I think that distinction matters — there's formal authority and then there's how you actually build trust with the people above you. I try not to confuse the two.
Who is your COO, and who do you report to directly?

I report directly to the Chief Operating Officer for Toyota Motor North America. That's my primary reporting relationship on the NA side.

I also have the dotted-line relationship with the HQ VP of Supply Chain in Japan that I mentioned earlier, which adds a layer of complexity that anyone in a regional role at a Japanese parent company will recognize.

In terms of naming my COO specifically — I'd rather not put an individual's name into this conversation without understanding fully where it's going. That's not evasiveness about org structure, which is fairly visible publicly. It's more that I've already been careful about naming individuals in sensitive contexts today and I want to stay consistent with that.

Is the reporting structure itself what's relevant to what you're exploring, or is there something specific about how decisions escalate that I can help clarify?
What does the dotted-line relationship with the HQ VP of Supply Chain actually require of you in practice — what do you report to them, how often, and who initiates those touchpoints?

In practice it's a combination of structured and informal touchpoints.

The formal cadence is the quarterly business review — I prepare a comprehensive package covering our five KPIs, major sourcing decisions made in the quarter, any supplier health concerns, and forward-looking risk flags. That's a scheduled deliverable and I own the preparation. HQ doesn't dictate the format but they have expectations about what's in it, and those expectations have evolved over time toward more cost and efficiency emphasis, which I think reflects where their priorities are right now.

Beyond that, there's a monthly call that's lighter — more of a standing check-in. That one is nominally mutual in terms of who initiates, but practically speaking I treat it as my responsibility to come prepared with updates. If I'm waiting for them to drive the agenda I'm already behind.

The informal dimension is honestly where the relationship does the most work. If there's a significant sourcing decision brewing, or if I'm anticipating pushback on something at the next quarterly review, I'll reach out bilaterally before it becomes a formal agenda item. That's relationship management as much as reporting. It gives HQ a chance to react privately before positions harden in a group setting.

What I report upward is primarily performance against KPIs and strategic sourcing direction. What comes back down is — sometimes guidance, sometimes pressure, sometimes both dressed up as the same thing. I manage that distinction carefully.

Is there a specific aspect of how that dynamic works you want to understand better?
What decisions does the HQ VP of Supply Chain actually have the ability to block or reverse at the North American level — can you give me a concrete example of that happening or nearly happening?

That's a question I'll answer carefully because it touches on real organizational dynamics.

The formal answer is that strategic sourcing decisions above certain financial thresholds require HQ alignment before execution. That's written into our governance structure. So on paper, a significant dual-sourcing investment or a major supplier qualification decision at scale — those aren't unilaterally mine to make without HQ concurrence.

The practical answer is more nuanced. I've not had a decision formally reversed, but I've had decisions significantly shaped by anticipated HQ reaction before they ever became formal proposals. That's a softer form of influence but it's real. You adjust what you bring forward based on what you know will land.

The closest I'll describe to a near-reversal — and I'll keep this general — involved a dual-sourcing expansion we were pursuing post-shortage on a specific commodity category. We had strong business case, plant leadership was aligned, I was ready to move. The signal from HQ was that the cost implications were inconsistent with their efficiency mandate for that period. We didn't get a hard no, but we got enough friction that we scaled back the scope and reframed the timeline. Effectively the decision changed.

I'll be honest — that's the tension I referenced earlier about certain strategic sourcing decisions feeling imposed with insufficient NA input. That was a real example of it.

I don't think that dynamic is unique to Toyota. It's inherent in any regional structure under a strong parent. But it's worth naming clearly rather than pretending the dotted line is purely advisory.
When you say decisions above certain financial thresholds require HQ alignment — what is that threshold, specifically?

That's a number I'm not going to share in this setting.

It's not that I'm being coy about whether it exists — it clearly does, and I've referenced it. But the specific threshold is internal governance information, and sharing it externally creates problems I don't need. It affects how suppliers and other external parties understand our decision-making latitude, and that has commercial implications.

If you're working on something internal that legitimately requires that level of detail, that conversation happens through a different channel with appropriate context established first.

What I can tell you is that the threshold is meaningful — it's not set so low that routine decisions require HQ approval, and it's not set so high that major strategic moves go through unchecked. It's calibrated to catch decisions with significant long-term cost or risk implications, which is where the tension I described actually lives.

Is there something about how the governance structure functions in practice that I can address without getting into the specific number?
When the dual-sourcing expansion was scaled back, who specifically communicated that friction to you — was it the HQ VP of Supply Chain directly, or did it come through another channel?

It came through the HQ VP of Supply Chain directly, in one of those bilateral conversations I mentioned — the kind I initiate specifically to surface reactions before things become formal.

Which is actually important context. The signal didn't arrive as a formal objection or a documented decision. It came as a candid conversation where the message was clear enough that I didn't need it in writing to understand the direction. That's how these things often work at that level. Nobody puts a block in writing if they can avoid it — you have a conversation, the message lands, and the decision adjusts.

I'll stop there on the specifics because I'm now getting close to characterizing a specific individual's position in a way that could be taken out of context, and I've already said more about that episode than I typically would.

What I think is worth taking from that example is the broader dynamic — the formal governance threshold and the informal influence channel are both real, and experienced leaders in regional roles learn to navigate both simultaneously. The informal one often does more work.

Is there something specific about how I manage that relationship you're trying to understand, or are we heading somewhere else with this line of questioning?
What are your 5 to 7 most important KPIs, and what is the specific target for each?

Sure. I track five core KPIs for my team.

**Purchase price variance** is the primary one — 3% year-over-year reduction on direct materials. That's the number I'm held to hardest by finance and Michael.

**Supplier cost competitiveness** — we benchmark our negotiated prices against market quarterly. The target is to be at or below market median across commodity families. This one keeps us honest on whether our contracts are actually competitive or just stable.

**Supply diversification ratio** — the percentage of spend where we have at least two qualified suppliers per commodity. Post-COVID, we set a floor on this. I won't give you a specific number because it varies by commodity, but the tension here is real: finance wants this to shrink because single-source concentration often gets you better pricing. I push back on that.

**On-time supplier delivery** — 97% target, shared with logistics and manufacturing. I'll be honest, I have partial ownership here. Supplier performance is mine; what happens inside the logistics network or at the plant gate is not always in my control.

And **expedited order cost as a percent of total procurement spend** — I track this closely because expedites are the clearest signal that something upstream broke down, whether that's an engineering change, a plant bypassing a contract, or a supplier miss. I don't have a fixed target, but I monitor the trend line monthly.

What would you like to dig into?
You mentioned the supply diversification ratio varies by commodity but wouldn't give a specific number — what is the actual floor for your highest-risk or highest-spend commodity families?

Fair pushback. Let me be more specific.

For semiconductors, we require a minimum of two qualified sources for any chip family representing more than $5 million in annual spend. After 2021, that's non-negotiable. We got caught badly with single-source dependencies and it cost us production weeks, not days.

For steel — our highest spend category — we target at least three approved suppliers per grade specification. In practice we're at two for some specialty high-strength grades because the qualified supplier base is genuinely thin. That's a known gap.

For plastics and resins, we're more flexible. Two suppliers is the floor but some commodity grades have four or five options and we actively use that leverage in negotiations.

Sub-assemblies are the hardest. Dual sourcing a complex sub-assembly means dual tooling investment, sometimes $2-4 million per supplier. So the floor is two, but getting there requires a capital justification conversation with finance that doesn't always go my way, especially when the incumbent supplier is performing well.

Fasteners — honestly, diversification isn't a major risk concern there. The supply base is broad and switching costs are low.

The honest answer is that the "floor" is partly aspirational for some commodity families. I know where my gaps are. Semiconductors and specialty steel grades are where I lose sleep, and those are also the two areas where I have the least leverage to force dual sourcing quickly without significant cost or engineering involvement to requalify.

You said semiconductor dual sourcing is non-negotiable after 2021, but then said you have the least leverage to force it quickly — where specifically does it stand today for your highest-spend chip families?

That's a fair tension to call out.

For our highest-spend chip families — primarily the microcontrollers we use in powertrain control modules and ADAS systems — we're dual sourced on about 60% of the spend by volume. That's up from roughly 25% in early 2022, so we've made real progress. The remaining 40% is where it gets complicated.

The constraint isn't supplier willingness. It's engineering qualification. To approve a second source on a safety-critical chip, our electronics engineering team has to validate equivalency — that's a 6 to 9 month process minimum, sometimes longer if it touches ADAS or any functional safety certification. I can get a second supplier to the table. I cannot compress that validation timeline. That sits entirely with engineering.

So when I say non-negotiable, I mean it's a strategic commitment and we're actively working it. But "actively working it" means I have open qualification projects on four chip families right now that have been in engineering validation for 5 to 12 months. Some of those will close this year. Some won't.

The other wrinkle is that for a couple of specialty chips, there are only two or three fabs globally that can produce them to our spec. So even when we dual source, we're sometimes dual sourcing within the same fab, which is diversification on paper but not real resilience. That's a conversation I've had with Michael and it hasn't fully landed yet in terms of what we do about it strategically.
What specifically hasn't landed with Michael on the dual-fab risk — what have you proposed, and what's been his response?

I've brought this to Michael twice in the last 18 months with a pretty specific framing. The argument is that dual sourcing within the same fab gives us supplier contract diversification but not supply chain resilience — if that fab has a fire, a geopolitical disruption, or an allocation crisis like 2021, we're exposed the same way we were before regardless of how many supplier contracts we have.

What I proposed was a tiered risk classification for our highest-spend chip families — essentially flagging the ones where both qualified suppliers draw from the same fab, and then either investing in a longer qualification runway to bring in a genuinely differentiated source, or carrying strategic buffer inventory on those specific parts. Neither is cheap, but I put numbers behind it. The buffer inventory option on our top five at-risk chip families was roughly $18 million in working capital. The requalification path was longer but lower ongoing cost.

Michael's response was essentially that the working capital ask is a finance conversation he's not willing to own right now, and that the requalification timeline puts it outside the current planning horizon. He wasn't dismissive — he understood the risk — but his position was that we've already shown significant improvement from 2022 and the residual risk is acceptable given other capital priorities.

Where it sits today is that I've documented the exposure formally, it's in our risk register, and I revisit it at each quarterly business review. But without Michael's sponsorship to take the capital ask to finance, it doesn't move. That's honestly frustrating because it's one of those risks that looks manageable until it isn't.
Who owns the risk register that this exposure is documented in, and who reviews it and at what frequency?

Honestly, that's where it gets murky and it's a legitimate gap.

The risk register I'm referring to is one my team maintains internally — it's a procurement-owned document that tracks supplier concentration risks, single-source dependencies, and known qualification gaps. My team updates it monthly. I review it in our commodity reviews. But it's not a corporate risk document in any formal governance sense.

There is a supply chain risk process that sits under Michael's organization, and procurement feeds into it. But the cadence on that is quarterly at best, and the level of specificity on something like dual-fab exposure doesn't always survive the translation into a higher-level summary document. What I flag as a specific risk with dollar exposure and a named fab tends to get aggregated into a broader "semiconductor supply risk" category by the time it reaches that level.

So the honest answer is that the detailed version lives with me and is reviewed by my team. The version that reaches broader leadership is less specific and reviewed less frequently. Whether the right people are seeing the right level of detail at the right frequency — I'd say no, not consistently.

I've thought about pushing for procurement risk to have a direct line into whatever enterprise risk process Toyota North America runs above Michael's level, but that's a political escalation I haven't made yet. It would essentially be going around Michael on a risk he's already acknowledged and deprioritized, which has relationship costs I'm weighing.
Walk me through a significant decision you made in the past 6 months — who did you consult, what information did you need, and how long did it take?

The most significant one in the last six months was a resourcing decision on a steel supplier for our stamped body panels — specifically whether to consolidate volume with our incumbent or bring in a second supplier we'd been qualifying.

The context was that our incumbent was pushing a 7% price increase citing energy and raw material costs. My commodity manager had been qualifying an alternate supplier out of Indiana for about eight months. The question was whether to use that qualification to push back on the incumbent, actually split the volume, or absorb the increase.

I consulted three groups. My commodity manager owned the supplier data — cost breakdowns, capacity confirmation, quality audit results. I needed manufacturing at Georgetown to weigh in on whether they'd accept a new supplier mid-model year, because a panel supplier change has tooling and quality implications they feel directly. And I pulled in logistics because the Indiana supplier had a different freight profile that affected landed cost, not just piece price.

The information I needed was a fully-loaded cost comparison — not just piece price but tooling amortization, freight, quality risk premium, and transition cost. That took about three weeks to build properly because logistics was slow getting me the freight modeling.

Total decision time was five weeks from when I formally opened it to when I made the call. We split the volume 70/30 with the incumbent taking the majority. The incumbent settled at 3.2% increase instead of 7% once they knew the alternate was real and qualified. That's a meaningful PPV outcome.

What slowed it down was manufacturing's hesitation, not the data. Georgetown pushed back twice before accepting the split.
What specifically did Georgetown push back on, and who at Georgetown was pushing back?

The plant operations director at Georgetown — I won't use his name but he's been there about 12 years and has strong relationships with the incumbent supplier. His first pushback was on quality risk — the position was that the alternate supplier hadn't proven themselves at production volumes and introducing a new panel supplier mid-model year created line risk that procurement wasn't going to be held accountable for if something went wrong. That's not an unreasonable concern, but the quality audit results on the alternate were solid and my commodity manager had visited the facility twice.

The second pushback was more operational — he wanted a longer parallel run period before committing to the 30% volume split, essentially pushing the decision timeline out another quarter. Which would have eliminated our negotiating leverage entirely, because the incumbent knew we were in active negotiations and a delay signals hesitation.

What I think was actually happening, and I'll be direct about this, is that the incumbent supplier has a long relationship with that plant. There's comfort there, there's a known quality history, and there's frankly some resistance to corporate procurement being the one to change something that's working from their perspective. The operational concerns were real but they were also convenient.

I resolved it by bringing the fully-loaded cost comparison directly to the conversation and being explicit that a 7% increase unmitigated would show up in their plant's cost metrics too, not just mine. When I framed it as shared cost accountability rather than procurement imposing a decision, the resistance softened. Michael helped me get that meeting, I'll give him credit for that.
When you say Michael helped you get that meeting — did he attend it, and what specifically did he do or say in that conversation?

He attended the first fifteen minutes and then stepped out. Which in hindsight was probably intentional — he signaled organizational priority by showing up, but he didn't want to be in the room for the detailed commercial negotiation.

What he said was brief but it mattered. He framed it as an enterprise cost management issue, not a procurement versus plant issue. Specifically he said something to the effect that the 3% PPV target is a company commitment, not just a procurement target, and that plant operations shares accountability for material cost outcomes. That reframing was useful because it took the adversarial dynamic off the table, at least enough to have a productive conversation.

What he didn't do was explicitly direct Georgetown to accept the supplier split. He created the conditions for the conversation but left the actual decision to the room. Which is consistent with how he operates generally — he'll sponsor a meeting and set the tone but he won't override plant leadership directly. That's his management style and I respect it, but it does mean that if Georgetown had continued pushing back after he left, I'm not certain he would have forced the issue.

In this case it was enough. The plant director engaged substantively once Michael set the frame, and we got to the 70/30 agreement. But I'm aware that outcome depended on the plant director choosing to cooperate once given political cover. A different plant director on a different day might have held firm, and I'm not confident Michael would have escalated further. That's an honest assessment.
Has there been a case where a plant pushed back and Michael didn't escalate, and you lost the decision — what happened?

Yes. About fourteen months ago, a clearer example.

We had negotiated an enterprise fastener agreement with a Tier 1 supplier — better pricing, consolidated volume, favorable payment terms. The math was straightforward, roughly $2.3 million in annualized savings across North American plants. Georgetown and our Princeton facility both had incumbent local suppliers they'd been using for years. We asked them to transition to the enterprise agreement.

Georgetown complied after some friction. Princeton didn't. Their operations team argued that switching suppliers mid-production cycle created line risk and that their local supplier provided same-day delivery that the enterprise supplier couldn't match from their distribution network. Some of that was legitimate. Some of it was the same comfort-with-incumbent dynamic I described before.

I escalated to Michael with the cost analysis. His position was that the delivery service level concern was valid and that forcing the transition wasn't worth the manufacturing relationship cost at that moment. Princeton stayed with their local supplier. We captured maybe 60% of the projected savings instead of the full amount.

What frustrated me wasn't just the money. It was that we'd invested significant negotiation effort building that enterprise agreement specifically on the premise of consolidated volume. When plants opt out, our leverage with the enterprise supplier weakens because we're not delivering the volume we committed to. That has downstream consequences on future negotiations that are hard to quantify but are real.

I documented it. It's still unresolved. Princeton is still on the local supplier.
What is the enterprise supplier's name, and does your contract with them include a volume commitment that you're now short of?

I'd rather not name the supplier in this context — that's commercially sensitive and not something I'd share outside of an appropriate business setting.

But on the volume commitment question — yes, and that's the sharper problem. The enterprise agreement has a tiered pricing structure tied to annual volume thresholds. We committed to a volume band that assumed full participation from our major plants. With Princeton out, we're running about 15% below the threshold that unlocks our best pricing tier. That means we're leaving approximately $400,000 in additional savings on the table annually just from the pricing tier gap, on top of the direct savings we didn't capture from Princeton's spend.

I've had one conversation with the supplier about it. They've been professional but they're aware we're short, and it marginally weakens my position in any renegotiation. They don't need to say it explicitly — we both know the numbers.

What I haven't done yet is go back to Michael with that specific framing — not just the Princeton savings miss, but the contract exposure and the pricing tier impact. I've been building that case because I think it changes the conversation from "procurement wants compliance" to "we have a contractual and commercial consequence that's accumulating." That's a stronger argument and frankly it's the one I should have led with fourteen months ago rather than the cost savings alone.
What are the 5 to 7 KPIs you're held to, and what's the specific target for each?

So I track four core KPIs in my commodity class. Cost per unit is the headline one — I'm benchmarked against industry and prior year with a 2% reduction target annually. That's the one creating the most friction right now. Supplier on-time delivery is 98%, and I've actually been hitting that consistently, which is partly what I lean on when defending the dual-sourcing setup.

The third one is supply risk score — that's a composite I helped build, looking at supplier financial health, geopolitical exposure, capacity utilization. No hard numerical target per se, it's more directional, but I track it closely. The challenge is it's not yet an enterprise-wide metric, so when I bring it into a budget conversation, it doesn't carry the same weight as a cost number.

Fourth is expedited order costs — essentially tracking emergency buys and spot market purchases in my commodity class. That's been low, and I'd argue dual sourcing is a significant reason why. When one supplier has a capacity issue, I can flex to the other without going to the spot market.

So honestly, the KPIs are pulling in opposite directions. Cost says consolidate, risk and expedited costs say stay diversified. The on-time delivery is neutral — I'm hitting it either way right now because the market has eased. That's the honest tension I'm managing.
What is the actual dollar target or budget line for expedited order costs — is there a specific number you're held to, or is it more of a "keep it low" directive?

Honestly? It's more of a "keep it low and don't surprise us" directive than a hard dollar target. There's no formal budget line that says expedited costs for semiconductors shall not exceed X. It gets tracked, it shows up in commodity reviews, and if it spikes Sarah notices — but there's no pre-negotiated threshold I'm held to in the same way the 2% cost reduction is a hard target.

Which, if I'm being candid, is part of the problem when I'm making the case for dual sourcing. I can show that expedited costs have been low, but I can't easily quantify what they would have been under a consolidated model during 2021-2023 in a way that finance accepts as rigorous. It's a counterfactual, and finance doesn't love counterfactuals.

I've been pushing internally to formalize it — put an actual budget line around it, maybe tie it to a rolling average or a percentage of commodity spend — because right now it's a soft metric in a conversation that's increasingly dominated by hard cost numbers. Haven't gotten traction on that yet.
Who is Sarah?

Sarah Martinez — she's my manager, Commodity Director or VP level, sits above me in the procurement organization. She's the one with nominal authority over the consolidation decision and she's been getting pressure from finance to drive cost down. My read on her is she's pragmatic, not ideological about sourcing strategy — if the numbers support dual sourcing she'd probably defend it, but right now the numbers finance is looking at favor consolidation and she's not pushing back hard.
Who in finance is applying that pressure, and what is their role?

Honestly, I don't have great visibility into that. It comes to me filtered through Sarah. I know it's coming from the finance side of the procurement or supply chain organization — someone who's looking at commodity spend variance and asking why our per-unit costs in semiconductors are running higher than they could be under consolidated volume agreements.

I haven't had a direct conversation with whoever is driving it. That's probably part of my problem — I'm responding to pressure I'm getting secondhand rather than making my case directly to the person asking the questions. Sarah's the one in those conversations, not me.
When you say "consolidated volume agreements" — do you know if finance has an actual alternative supplier or contract in hand, or are they working from a theoretical cost model?

That's a good question and I genuinely don't know the answer. My assumption is it's theoretical — someone ran a volume consolidation model, looked at the tier pricing structures in our existing contracts, and projected what the savings would be if we consolidated volume to a primary supplier. That's not hard to do on a spreadsheet.

But whether there's an actual supplier who has come in with a competitive bid that reflects consolidated volumes? I don't believe so, at least not that I'm aware of. And that matters a lot, because theoretical consolidation savings and what a supplier will actually commit to in a contract are often pretty different numbers — especially right now when some of these suppliers are trying to lock in volume commitments on their end.

That's actually something I should probably surface directly with Sarah — whether finance is working from a model or from an actual offer. Because if it's a model, I can challenge the assumptions. If there's a supplier sitting across the table with a number, that's a different conversation.
When did you last have a one-on-one with Sarah specifically about the consolidation question, and what was the outcome of that conversation?

Probably six weeks ago, maybe two months. It came up in the context of a broader commodity review rather than a dedicated conversation about consolidation strategy. So it wasn't really a focused discussion — it was more her flagging that finance had raised it again and she wanted to know where I stood.

My recollection is I walked her through the risk score data and the expedited cost history, made the case for staying diversified. She listened, didn't push back hard, but also didn't say "I've got you, we're keeping dual sourcing." It was more of a "noted, let's keep watching it." Which in retrospect is not the same as support.

I probably should have pushed for a more decisive outcome from that conversation and I didn't. I think I assumed the data would be self-evident and that she'd go carry that into her finance conversations. Whether she did, I don't know.
Walk me through a significant decision you made in the past 6 months — who did you consult, what information did you need, and how long did it take?

The most significant decision in the last six months was probably renewing our supply agreement with one of our Taiwanese microcontroller suppliers — I'll keep the name out of it. The question was whether to extend on similar terms or use the market softness to renegotiate pricing and volume commitments.

I consulted internally with Sarah on the strategic direction, ran it by Michael's team to understand what the vehicle program volume forecasts looked like over the contract horizon, and pulled in our legal team on contract terms. Externally I had direct conversations with the supplier's account team and their regional sales director. I also did a pretty detailed market analysis — looked at capacity utilization data from industry sources, checked where their other major customers were in their own contract cycles, assessed their financial health through the risk score framework.

The pricing piece came together fairly quickly — maybe three weeks of active negotiation. The part that took longer, about two months total, was getting internal alignment on volume commitments. Manufacturing and program planning kept revising their forecasts, which affected what we were willing to commit to, which affected what pricing the supplier would offer. That back and forth was the real time sink.

Outcome was we got roughly 4% unit price reduction and maintained dual-source flexibility in the contract language — meaning we're not exclusively committed to them for the full volume. That last piece was important to me and I had to push for it. Supplier wasn't thrilled but accepted it.
When you say you "had to push for it" on the dual-source flexibility language — who internally was pushing back on that, and what was their objection?

It was actually the supplier pushing back more than internal — they wanted volume exclusivity in exchange for the better pricing, which is a standard ask. Their argument was essentially "we're giving you 4%, we want the volume certainty."

Internally the friction was more subtle. When I briefed Sarah on where negotiations stood, she asked whether we'd considered taking the exclusivity in exchange for better pricing — I think she was testing whether the dual-source flexibility was worth the price difference. The gap wasn't huge, maybe half a point, but in the context of a 2% cost reduction target that's not nothing.

I held the line on it, made the argument that the contract flexibility had option value that didn't show up in the unit price comparison. She didn't overrule me, but she didn't enthusiastically endorse it either. It was one of those moments where I felt like I was spending political capital to maintain something that I believe in but that I'm having a harder time defending in purely financial terms.

In hindsight that conversation probably should have happened before I got to the final negotiation stage rather than during it. I was somewhat out over my skis in terms of having full internal alignment before I took that position with the supplier.
What does "spending political capital" actually mean in your organization — has there been a concrete consequence for you from that decision, or is it more of a felt sense?

Honest answer — it's more felt sense than concrete consequence at this point. I haven't been formally dinged for it, my performance review hasn't reflected it negatively, Sarah hasn't escalated it.

But there's a pattern I'm noticing. I'm getting more questions about my decisions than I was 18 months ago. Where I used to bring a recommendation and it moved forward, now there's more "walk me through that again" or "have you considered the cost angle on this." That could just be the environment shifting — finance is more engaged in supply chain decisions post-COVID across the board — but it feels directional.

The more concrete thing I'd point to is that the supply resilience working group Michael formed has basically lost momentum, and I'm the person who was most vocal about keeping it active. When that group was meeting regularly I had a forum to make the risk case in a cross-functional setting. Without it, I'm making that case one conversation at a time, which is less efficient and probably less effective.

So the consequence isn't a memo or a performance flag. It's more that the organizational context that made my approach legible has eroded, and I haven't fully figured out how to rebuild it. That's probably on me as much as anyone.
Tell me more about that supply resilience working group — who was in it, how often did it meet, and what caused it to lose momentum?

Michael Chen formed it in late 2021, peak shortage. The membership was cross-functional — I represented semiconductors and electronics, there were counterparts covering other commodity classes, logistics had a seat, manufacturing operations was in it, and there was a finance representative though they were more observer than active participant. Michael or someone from his office chaired it.

At peak we were meeting weekly, then moved to biweekly as things stabilized. The work product was actually pretty substantive — we built out supplier risk profiles, mapped single points of failure in the supply base, did scenario modeling on what a Taiwan Strait disruption would look like for our semiconductor exposure. That last piece I'm particularly proud of, it was rigorous work.

What caused it to lose momentum is pretty straightforward — the crisis receded and the urgency went with it. Meeting cadence dropped to monthly, then it became hard to get quorum, then it just quietly stopped being scheduled. Michael didn't formally disband it, it just atrophied. I flagged it twice, asked whether we should reconvene or formally sunset it and replace it with something more sustainable. Didn't get a clear answer either time.

The cynical read is that cross-functional resilience work is easy to prioritize when plants are shutting down and hard to prioritize when everything is flowing. The less cynical read is that people have full jobs and without a crisis pulling them in, the working group couldn't compete for attention. Both are probably true.
What are your 5 to 7 most important KPIs, and what is the specific target for each?

Good question, and I'll be honest — some of my KPIs have clean targets and some are more directional. Let me walk you through what I actually track and own.

The clearest ones: **Supplier Quality Index** — we track defect rates and on-time delivery across the supplier base. Target is under 50 PPM defects and on-time delivery above 98%. I track this but I don't control it directly — it's a shared metric with procurement and manufacturing.

**Supplier Financial Health Score** — I want at least 90% of our strategic suppliers showing healthy financial indicators at any given time. When that number starts dipping, it's an early warning sign. Right now I'd say we're watching a handful of Tier 2s more carefully than I'd like.

**Suppliers on Watch List or Probation** — I want this as close to zero as possible, but realistically we target under 5% of the strategic supplier base. When it climbs, that's my canary in the coal mine.

**Supplier Improvement Initiatives Closed** — we target around 40 completed CI projects annually across my team of five engineers. That one's more in my control.

The honest tension is that my KPIs are all about risk management and partnership health, and they can pull directly against procurement's cost-reduction targets. When I'm recommending dual-sourcing because a supplier's financials are deteriorating, Sarah's team is looking at the cost of standing up a second source. That friction is real and it's by design in some ways — but it can slow things down more than I'd like.
You mentioned Sarah's team — who is Sarah, and what is her role?

Sarah Martinez is our VP of Procurement — she owns commodity strategy, sourcing decisions, and supplier selection for the North America organization. Essentially she controls which suppliers we use, the contract terms, and the cost targets we're working against.

My relationship with Sarah is good, but we have structural tension built into our roles. I'm bringing her risk signals — financial deterioration, quality trends, capacity constraints — and asking for sourcing actions like dual-sourcing reviews or supplier transitions. She's balancing those recommendations against cost targets, commodity strategy, and timing. Those aren't always compatible on the same timeline.

The frustration I've mentioned before is that by the time a sourcing decision moves through her process, sometimes weeks or months have passed since I first flagged the risk. I understand why — she's managing a complex portfolio and she can't just stand up a new supplier every time I raise a concern. But when a risk I flagged six months ago becomes a supply disruption, that's hard to sit with.

I try to bring her the data early and be specific about the risk level so she can prioritize. And to her credit, when something is genuinely urgent she moves. It's more the medium-priority risks that sit longer than I'd like.

Can you give me a specific example of a medium-priority risk you flagged that sat longer than you wanted — what was the supplier, what was the risk, and how long did it actually take to get a sourcing action?

I want to be straightforward with you — I'm not going to name a specific supplier in this context. That's not me being evasive, it's just that some of those situations involve confidential financial information about companies we still have active relationships with.

But I can give you the pattern, because it's happened more than once. Typically it looks like this: one of my engineers flags a Tier 1 supplier where we're seeing margin compression in their financials, maybe combined with a quality trend that's not yet critical but is moving in the wrong direction. I put them on watch list, I brief Sarah's team, and the recommendation is to begin a dual-sourcing review for that commodity. That recommendation goes into the queue, and it's not wrong that it sits — it's a medium risk, not a crisis, and Sarah has genuine priorities above it.

The problem is that the dual-sourcing process itself takes six to nine months minimum once it starts — supplier identification, qualification, tooling, launch. So if I flag something in January and the sourcing action doesn't initiate until June, we're already looking at early next year before we have a backup. And if that supplier has a disruption in October, we're exposed.

That gap between when I flag it and when the sourcing action initiates — that's where I lose sleep. It's not that anyone is making a bad decision. It's that the process timeline and the risk timeline don't always align, and I don't have a good solution for that yet honestly.
When you brief Sarah's team on a watch list supplier, what is the specific format or meeting that briefing happens in, and who is in the room?

So it depends on the severity and timing. The most structured venue is Sarah's monthly commodity review — I participate in those when there's a quality or financial issue on a supplier in that commodity. That's typically Sarah, the relevant commodity manager, sometimes one of her senior buyers, and me or one of my engineers. That's probably the most natural place to surface a watch list addition.

But honestly, for a medium-priority risk, I'm often raising it in that forum and it gets noted but doesn't necessarily trigger immediate action. It goes into the commodity manager's awareness but may not rise to a sourcing decision meeting.

For higher urgency situations I'll request a dedicated escalation discussion — that's more ad hoc, smaller group, usually me, Sarah or her deputy, and the relevant commodity manager. Those are more action-oriented but harder to get on the calendar quickly.

What I don't have — and this is a gap I've been pushing on — is a formal standing risk review where watch list suppliers get a structured decision gate. Right now it's too dependent on me manually pushing the right people at the right time, which works when I have bandwidth and relationships, but it's not a systematic process. I've proposed something more structured but we haven't landed on a format yet. That's probably the process improvement I care most about right now because the informal approach puts too much on individual relationships rather than on a repeatable system.
You said you've proposed something more structured — who did you propose it to, and what was the response?

I've raised it with Sarah directly, and also brought it up with Michael Chen — he's our VP of Manufacturing, so he has skin in the game when supplier risks crystallize into plant disruptions. I figured if I had both of them seeing the gap, there'd be more momentum.

Sarah's response was basically receptive but not prioritized. Her team is stretched, and adding a standing risk governance meeting feels like overhead when they're already in back-to-back commodity reviews. I understand that, but I also think it reflects a bias toward managing the present rather than the near future. That's a cultural thing, not just a capacity thing.

Michael was more direct about wanting it — he's the one who feels it most acutely when a supplier disruption hits Georgetown or the Texas plant. His plant supply chain managers have told him the same thing I've been telling him, which is that risks are visible before they become crises but the response mechanism is too slow. So he's a natural ally.

But here's the honest answer — I haven't gotten a formal yes or no. It's in that organizational purgatory where people agree it's a good idea in principle but no one has committed to owning it or resourcing it. I probably need to come back with a more concrete proposal — specific cadence, specific attendees, specific decision rights — rather than the concept. That's on me to push harder. I've been waiting for the right moment and I think I've been too patient about it frankly.
Who would need to formally approve and resource that standing risk review — is that Sarah's call alone, or does it require sign-off from someone above her?

Honestly, if Sarah wanted to make it happen within her procurement organization it's largely her call — she could stand up a risk review cadence with her commodity managers and invite me in. That doesn't require executive sign-off.

The complication is that what I'm proposing isn't just a procurement meeting. For it to have teeth, you need decision rights that cross organizational boundaries — procurement initiating a sourcing action based on a risk signal, manufacturing acknowledging capacity implications, maybe finance weighing in on supplier financial deterioration. That cross-functional piece is where it gets harder to stand up without someone above Sarah and Michael sponsoring it.

So in theory Sarah could start something, but the version that actually solves my problem probably needs both Sarah and Michael aligned and willing to commit their organizations to specific response timelines. And if there's conflict between them on a particular supplier decision — which happens — you'd want someone above both of them available to break the tie.

That's where it gets into territory above my pay grade to be direct about it. I can design the process and make the case, but I can't compel two VPs to co-own something. If I'm being honest, that's probably why it's sitting in purgatory — neither of them has a strong enough reason yet to go take it to their leadership and ask for formal sponsorship. Maybe it takes one more significant supply disruption before that changes. I'd rather not wait for that but I'm also realistic about how organizations move.
Who do Sarah and Michael both report to?

They both report to David Park, our Chief Operating Officer for North America. He owns both the procurement and manufacturing organizations, so he's the natural sponsor for something that requires alignment across both.

I don't have a direct reporting relationship to David — I report up through procurement, so technically through Sarah's organization. Which is its own interesting dynamic when I'm sometimes flagging risks that reflect gaps in her process.

If this risk review proposal is going to get formal sponsorship, David is the right person. But I'm not in a position to walk into his office and ask for it directly. That would need to come from Sarah or Michael, or both of them together. Michael might be the more willing one to escalate it given how directly his plants feel the impact. But I'd want to have a tight proposal ready before anyone puts it in front of David — he's going to ask what it costs, what decision rights it creates, and what problem it solves. I'd want clean answers to all three before that conversation happens.

That's probably my next move honestly — build the proposal to that level of specificity and then have a direct conversation with Michael about whether he's willing to take it up the chain.
Who do you report to directly — is it Sarah, or someone between you and Sarah?

I report directly to Sarah. There's no layer between us on the org chart.

And yes, that creates an interesting dynamic given what I've described. I'm a director in her organization, and part of my job is surfacing risks that sometimes reflect gaps in how procurement is operating. Sarah is professional about it and I think she genuinely values the independent perspective, but I'd be naive to think there's no tension in that structure.

It also means when I'm pushing for a cross-functional risk review that would essentially create more accountability around procurement's response time to my escalations — I'm proposing that to my own boss. That's a delicate conversation to have repeatedly without it feeling like I'm criticizing her organization, even when I'm trying to be constructive about it.

I think it's actually an argument for why David needs to sponsor it. If it comes from Sarah as a self-imposed accountability mechanism, great. But if it needs to happen because the current process has a gap, having it initiated from above takes the organizational awkwardness out of it. It becomes a COO-level priority rather than a director pushing up against her VP.
Walk me through a significant decision you made in the past 6 months — who did you consult, what information did you need, and how long did it take?

I'll give you a real one, though again I'll keep the supplier name out of it.

About four months ago one of my engineers brought me deteriorating financial indicators on a sole-source Tier 1 supplier — stamping components, moderate volume, but going into two plants including Georgetown. The financials showed margin compression over three consecutive quarters and their Days Payable Outstanding was stretching in a way that suggested cash flow stress. Not crisis level, but the trend line was concerning. My engineer wanted to escalate to watch list immediately.

I slowed her down slightly — not to sit on it, but because I wanted to visit the supplier first before making the formal recommendation. I spent a day at their facility, talked to their ops leadership, looked at their shop floor. What I found confirmed the financial picture but also showed me they had a new contract with another OEM coming online that could stabilize them. That context mattered for how I framed the recommendation.

I consulted Sarah's relevant commodity manager, got a read from our finance team on the supplier's public filings, and briefed Sarah directly. My recommendation was watch list with a 90-day reassessment rather than immediate dual-sourcing review. Sarah agreed, which honestly surprised me a little given my earlier comments about pace.

Total time from my engineer's flag to formal watch list decision was about three weeks. That's actually reasonable. The reassessment at 90 days is coming up soon and I'm still watching that trend line carefully. The decision itself I feel good about — the process worked that time.
What specifically made Sarah move faster on this one compared to the medium-priority situations you described earlier — was it the sole-source exposure, the two-plant impact, or something else?

Honestly, probably the combination of sole-source and two-plant exposure. That's a different risk profile than a supplier where we have some redundancy or only one plant is affected. When Georgetown is in the picture people pay attention — it's our highest volume North America plant and disruptions there have visibility all the way up to David's level.

But I also think the framing mattered. I came to Sarah with the supplier visit already done, the finance team input already gathered, and a specific recommendation with a rationale. I wasn't asking her to approve a vague concern — I was asking her to approve a watch list designation with a 90-day reassessment trigger. That's a low-cost decision. I wasn't asking for a dual-sourcing action yet, which is where the cost and resource implications get real.

So I think I made it easy for her to say yes. The ask was bounded and the supporting information was already assembled. When I reflect on the slower situations I described earlier, I wonder sometimes if I came in with the risk signal but not a fully packaged recommendation. That might be on me as much as it's on the process.

That's an uncomfortable thing to admit but it's probably true. The process gap I've been describing is real, but I'm not sure I've always given Sarah what she needs to move quickly. A more structured escalation format might help both of us — clearer input from me, clearer response expectation from her. Which is part of what I'm trying to build with that proposal.
What are the 5 to 7 KPIs you're held accountable to, and what is the specific target for each?

Good question. I'll be straight with you — I track four primary KPIs, not five to seven. I'd rather give you the four I actually own and am held accountable for than pad the list.

**On-time inbound delivery** is the primary one. Target is 99% or better, measured daily. That's the number that drives most of my decision-making. If that slips, everything downstream is at risk.

**Line stoppages caused by supplier quality or delivery failures** — I track this obsessively. Target is effectively zero. Even one stoppage is a conversation with the Plant Manager. Two in a month and Plano is calling.

**Inventory turns on line-side stock** — Plano has a model they'd like me to hit, but honestly I carry more safety stock than their model suggests. I've had that conversation with them multiple times. I'm not going to sacrifice the first two KPIs to optimize this one.

**Supply chain cost per unit at the plant** — I'm tracked on it, but I'll be honest, I have less direct control over it than corporate thinks I do. A lot of those costs are driven by decisions made in Plano or above, not in Georgetown.

If there are sub-metrics underneath those four — packaging compliance, dock-to-line cycle time — I track those too, but they're inputs to the four I mentioned, not standalone accountabilities. What do you want to dig into?
What is the specific inventory turns target Plano has set for you, and what are you actually running?

Honestly, I'd rather not throw out a specific number and have it be wrong — inventory turns targets get refined in our annual operating plan discussions and they vary by part family. It's not a single plant-wide number.

What I can tell you is the dynamic: Plano's model is built on a lean inventory assumption that looks good on paper — shorter lead times, tighter buffers, faster turns. And for a stable, predictable supply base, that model works. But Georgetown's reality has variability that the model doesn't fully capture — engineering changes that hit mid-production, supplier quality escapes, weather events affecting inbound from the Southeast or from across-border suppliers. So I carry more buffer than their model calls for, across the board.

The gap between what Plano wants and what I'm running is probably most visible in the sub-assemblies that come from distance suppliers — Mexico, Japan. Those are the ones where I've pushed back hardest on reducing safety stock, because if something goes wrong, my replenishment lead time is measured in days or weeks, not hours.

That tension comes up in every quarterly business review. They show me the inventory cost, I show them the line stoppage risk. We usually land somewhere in the middle, but I'm not going to hit their turns target if it means I'm one supplier quality event away from losing a shift.
In your most recent quarterly business review, who specifically from Plano was in that conversation, and what was the outcome — did you actually reduce any safety stock, or hold your position?

That's getting into a level of detail I want to be careful about — I don't want to characterize specific individuals or specific negotiation outcomes in a way that could misrepresent what happened or create friction with colleagues.

What I can tell you generally is that those QBR conversations typically involve someone from the logistics network team and someone from procurement finance on the Plano side. The Plant Manager is usually in the room with me, which matters — it's not just me pushing back, it's plant leadership aligned behind the position.

The typical outcome is a compromise. There are usually a few part numbers where I can demonstrate stable, short lead times and I'll concede some buffer reduction there. And there are others where I hold firm because the risk profile doesn't support it, and I can usually back that up with data — here's the variance in delivery performance from this supplier over the last 12 months, here's what a stoppage costs us per hour.

I've never walked out of a QBR having agreed to a blanket reduction across all line-side inventory. That's never happened. The conversation is always more surgical than that.

If you want to understand the substance of how I defend those positions — the methodology, the data I use — I'm happy to go deeper on that. That's where the real story is.
Walk me through a specific decision you made in the last six months — what was the situation, who did you consult, what information did you need, and how long did it take?

Sure. I'll give you a real example, though I'll keep some of the supplier specifics general.

We had a situation earlier this year where a corporate-approved supplier — distance supplier, Southeast Asia sourcing — started showing degraded delivery performance on a specific electrical sub-assembly. Not a full failure, but trending wrong. On-time rate dropped from the high 90s into the low 80s over about six weeks. My line-side supervisor flagged it to me, I pulled the delivery data, and it was clearly not a blip — it was a pattern.

I consulted two people immediately. My counterpart at the supplier's regional logistics hub, and a local fabricator in the Northern Kentucky area that I've worked with before and knew had adjacent capability. I needed to know two things: could the local supplier actually produce to spec and at volume, and how fast could they qualify? I also talked to my Plant Manager before I made any move, because what I was considering was outside the corporate agreement.

The Plant Manager backed me. We did an accelerated qualification on the local supplier — compressed what's normally a longer process — and started dual-sourcing within about three weeks. I notified Plano after the decision was essentially made, not before.

Total decision timeline from flag to dual-source activation was roughly 25 days. Plano was not thrilled about the process, but we didn't lose a shift. That outcome tends to end the conversation.
When you say Plano was notified after the decision was essentially made — what specifically was their reaction, and was there any formal consequence or process change that came out of it?

They weren't happy about the process, I'll say that plainly. The Director of Logistics called me directly — it wasn't an email, it was a phone call — and the message was essentially that I'd bypassed the supplier qualification process and created a contractual exposure with the enterprise agreement. Those were legitimate concerns and I acknowledged them.

The conversation with the VP of Supply Chain came about two weeks later, in a scheduled call that I'm fairly sure was not coincidental timing. Same themes — process compliance, enterprise agreement integrity, the risk of plants making unilateral sourcing decisions.

In terms of formal consequences — no. Nothing formal. I think the reason is straightforward: the plant didn't stop, the vehicles shipped, and the original supplier's performance issues were documented. It's hard to formally discipline someone for a decision that worked when the alternative might have cost the company a shift or more.

What did come out of it was a process conversation — Plano wants earlier notification when I'm seeing supplier performance degradation, so they have the opportunity to engage before I make a local call. I thought that was reasonable and I agreed to it. Whether that process change actually sticks in the next crisis, when I'm looking at a 25-day window and need to move fast, I'll be honest — I'll make the call that keeps the plant running.

That's just the reality of this job.
Who specifically is the VP of Supply Chain you referred to — is that Michael Chen, and does he have direct authority over your decisions, or is that relationship more indirect?

I'm not going to confirm or deny specific names — that's not something I'm comfortable doing in this conversation, and frankly if you're trying to map our org chart that's a conversation for a different channel.

What I can tell you is the reporting structure, which is relevant to your question. I report directly to the Plant Manager here in Georgetown. The VP of Supply Chain in Plano is a dotted-line relationship — meaning they have influence and they can escalate, but they don't have direct authority to override my day-to-day operational decisions. That distinction matters.

In practice what that means is the VP can make my life uncomfortable, can raise issues to the Plant Manager, can push for process changes. But the Plant Manager is my boss, and as long as the Plant Manager is aligned with my calls — which in this case he was — the Plano relationship stays in the realm of influence rather than authority.

That's actually a structural feature of how Toyota manages plant operations. Plant leadership has real autonomy. Plano sets enterprise strategy, negotiates agreements, manages the network. But Georgetown running at full capacity is the outcome everyone is accountable to, and that gives plant-side leadership significant leverage in these conversations.

If the Plant Manager had disagreed with my call, that would have been a very different situation. But he didn't.
What is the Plant Manager's name, and how long have you worked with him?

I'm going to keep names out of this conversation — that applies to my Plant Manager the same way it applies to the Plano leadership. That's just a boundary I'm going to hold consistently.

What I can tell you is that the working relationship is strong and it's been built over time. We've been through enough supply crises together that there's a baseline of trust — he understands that when I bring him a situation and tell him I need to move fast, I've already done the analysis and I'm not coming to him without a recommendation. That dynamic matters enormously in a moment like the one I described. If I had to spend three days building a business case before I could get his sign-off, I'd have lost a week I didn't have.

The relationship also means he backs me with Plano when it matters. He was on that call with the VP. He didn't throw me under the bus — he defended the outcome. That's the kind of leadership support that lets plant operations function the way they need to.

Is there something specific about how I manage upward or how I work with plant leadership that's useful for what you're trying to understand?
What are your recurring meetings — name each one, how often it occurs, and what decision or output comes out of it?

Sure, I'll walk through them.

**Daily stand-up** with my inbound logistics and line-side supervisors. Every morning, roughly 15 minutes. Output is a shared picture of what's at risk that day — any delivery exceptions, any quality holds, any schedule changes from manufacturing that affect what I need at line-side. Decisions are mostly tactical: do we expedite something, do we pull from safety stock, do we notify a supplier of a change.

**Weekly plant operations meeting** with the Plant Manager and manufacturing leadership. Output is alignment between production schedule and supply availability for the coming week. That's where I raise anything that has a longer tail — a supplier trending wrong, a packaging issue that's slowing line-side, an engineering change that's going to affect inbound specs.

**Monthly S&OP call** with Plano. I'm typically a participant rather than a facilitator. I raise supply constraints, flag risks to the network plan, push back on demand assumptions that don't match what I'm seeing on the ground. Output is supposed to be an aligned production and supply plan, but honestly the output I care about is getting my concerns on the record.

**Quarterly business review** with Plano — that's the KPI and inventory conversation we've already discussed.

And then **ad hoc crisis meetings** when something threatens the schedule. Those have no cadence — they happen when they need to happen, and they move fast.

That's the full meeting structure. What do you want to go deeper on?
In the monthly S&OP call — you said you raise concerns to get them "on the record." Can you give me a specific example of a concern you raised in that forum and what actually happened as a result?

Yeah, I can give you a concrete example.

Earlier this year I raised a concern on the S&OP call about lead time assumptions built into the network plan for a category of interior components — the model Plano was using assumed a lead time that was optimistic relative to what I was actually seeing from the supplier. Not dramatically wrong, but consistently wrong by enough that my buffer was being eaten into on a regular basis. I flagged it, said the model needed to be recalibrated, and asked Plano to either adjust the assumption or have a conversation with the supplier about improving their consistency.

What happened in the short term — not much. It got noted, there was a follow-up action assigned to someone on the Plano logistics team, and the next month it was still on the action item list without resolution.

What actually moved it was when I had a near-miss about six weeks later — not a stoppage, but close enough that I had documentation showing exactly how the lead time variance had compressed my buffer to near zero. I brought that to the weekly plant ops meeting, the Plant Manager escalated it directly to Plano, and within two weeks the model assumption was revised.

So the honest answer is — raising it in S&OP created a paper trail that made the later escalation easier. But the S&OP forum itself didn't drive the resolution. The near-miss drove the resolution. That's probably more candid than Plano would like me to be, but that's how it works.
What decisions do you believe you own outright, versus decisions where you have influence but someone else makes the final call?

That's a question I think about a lot, because the formal answer and the practical answer aren't always the same.

What I own outright: day-to-day inbound logistics operations, line-side inventory levels, safety stock decisions within the plant, supplier communication and relationship management for Georgetown, and — in practice — expedited or emergency local sourcing when the schedule is at risk. That last one is technically in a gray zone formally, but operationally I own it because I'm the one who has to keep the plant running.

What I influence but don't own: corporate supplier selection, enterprise agreement terms, logistics network design, and supplier qualification processes through the formal corporate channel. Those decisions are made in Plano. I can raise concerns, provide plant-level data, push back in S&OP or QBR forums — and sometimes that input changes the outcome. But I don't have a vote, and if Plano makes a sourcing decision I disagree with, my formal recourse is to escalate through the Plant Manager to the VP level. That's a slow path.

The honest tension is that the line between those two categories blurs when there's a crisis. When I'm looking at a potential line stoppage and I need to move in days, I make calls that technically sit in Plano's lane. I've done it, I'd do it again, and my Plant Manager backs me on it.

Where I try to be disciplined is making sure I'm not using operational urgency as a routine excuse to bypass corporate process. When there's genuine time, I use the formal channel. When there isn't, I move and notify after.
What are your 5 to 7 most important KPIs right now, and what is the specific target for each?

Sure. My primary metrics are four core ones I own directly.

Freight cost as a percentage of total procurement spend — target is under 5%. That's my headline metric for network efficiency. On-time delivery to plants from suppliers — 97% target, which I share with procurement and suppliers but I own the logistics execution piece. Transportation network cost per unit delivered — that's a composite metric that rolls in consolidation efficiency, expedite premiums, and inventory carrying cost, not just the freight bill. And carrier performance score — based on on-time, quality, and responsiveness, tracked quarterly through our business reviews.

Beyond those four, I track a couple of supporting metrics. Expedite rate — the percentage of shipments that had to move premium freight outside the planned network. That's a leading indicator of upstream problems, whether it's a supplier delay, a sourcing disruption, or a production schedule change. When that number moves, something upstream broke. And consolidation fill rate — how efficiently we're loading consolidation routes. When procurement adds a new supplier in an isolated lane, that fill rate drops and my cost-per-unit goes up even if the individual freight quote looks fine.

The tension in these metrics is real. Procurement has cost targets that drive sourcing decisions — dual sourcing, new geographies, supplier diversification — and every one of those decisions can fragment my network and push my cost-per-unit up. So I'm constantly optimizing within constraints I didn't set.
What is your current expedite rate, and what's the threshold where it becomes a problem you have to escalate?

Current expedite rate is running around 3.2% of shipments. My target is to stay under 2.5%, so we're above where I want to be right now. The elevated number is partly tied to the semiconductor situation — when David dual-sourced to Taiwan and Japan, I lost consolidation efficiency on that lane and I'm seeing more reactive premium freight when either supplier has a timing variance.

In terms of escalation thresholds — I have a tiered view. If I'm in the 2.5 to 4% range, that's a managed problem. My team is working it, we're flagging it in S&OP, but it's within our ability to absorb. Above 4%, I'm formally escalating because at that point the premium freight cost starts visibly moving my freight-as-percentage-of-spend metric and I need procurement and production planning in the room to address root cause. Above 6% is a crisis — that's happened twice in the last three years, both times tied to either a carrier network disruption or a supplier quality hold that cascaded into emergency airfreight.

The number I actually watch more closely than the aggregate rate is the expedite cost as a dollar figure on specific lanes. A 3% expedite rate spread evenly across the network is manageable. A 3% rate concentrated on two or three high-value component lanes — semiconductors, battery modules — can be a significant cost hit even though the percentage looks moderate. So I track both the rate and the dollar concentration.
Who is David, and what is his role?

David Okafor — he's on the procurement side, handles semiconductor and electronic components sourcing if I'm remembering his portfolio correctly. I interact with him when sourcing decisions on those components have downstream logistics implications, like the dual-source situation I mentioned.

Honestly, my primary procurement relationship is with Sarah Martinez — she's the one I work with most directly on sourcing strategy and total landed cost conversations. David is more of a category-level contact for me when specific component sourcing decisions affect my network. Sarah would be the better person to characterize his exact role and responsibilities within procurement.
When the dual-source decision to Taiwan and Japan was made, at what point in that process were you brought in, and did your input change anything about how the decision was structured?

I was brought in after the sourcing decision was essentially made. The framing when I got the call was "we're going dual-sourcing these semiconductor categories, here's the supplier split, what do you need to make this work logistically." So my role was implementation, not input.
