So even when my analysis points in a different direction, the conversational momentum is already around his framing by the time I've finished. I've thought about whether that structural position is working against me — whether I should be pushing for a separate analytics slot where my findings stand alone before planning's recommendation is anchored in the room.

I haven't made that ask yet. I'm trying to figure out if that's the right move politically, or whether it creates unnecessary friction with David.
Have you raised that specific structural question — getting a separate slot — with Michael Chen directly?

No, I haven't. Not directly.

I've had conversations with Michael about adoption challenges generally, and he's been supportive in a broad sense — he's said the analytics capability is important, that he wants the organization using it more. But I haven't made the specific ask: "I need my own agenda slot in S&OP so my analysis isn't subordinated to planning's framing before the room has a chance to evaluate it independently."

Part of my hesitation is that I don't want it to come across as a territorial move against David. David and I have a decent working relationship, and I don't want to blow that up over a meeting structure question. But I also recognize that's partly me talking myself out of advocating for something that would probably improve outcomes.

The other part is honestly uncertainty about how Michael would receive it. He's TPS-trained, very relationship-oriented, and I sometimes can't read whether he sees the structural issue the way I see it — as a decision quality problem — or whether he'd see it as me being impatient or not understanding how the organization works.

So the honest answer is I've been waiting until I had a stronger track record to stand on before making that ask. The demand softening example I described is probably the clearest evidence I have that the current structure isn't working. I think I'm close to the point where I need to have that conversation directly with Michael rather than hoping the structural problem resolves itself.
What does your reporting relationship to Michael actually look like in practice — how often do you meet one-on-one, and what do those conversations typically cover?

We have a scheduled one-on-one every two weeks, and it usually runs about 45 minutes. In practice it's probably more like monthly in terms of ones that actually happen without being rescheduled — he's a busy person and these are the meetings that get moved when something operational comes up.

When we do meet, the conversations tend to cover three things. Status on ongoing analytics projects, any escalations where I need his awareness or air cover, and occasionally broader strategic questions about where the analytics function should be going. That third category is the most valuable to me but probably gets the least time.

What I notice is that Michael engages most readily when I bring him a concrete operational problem where analytics is already showing clear value. He's less naturally drawn into the more abstract conversations about organizational adoption or decision-making processes. So I've learned to lead with specific examples rather than systemic observations.

The honest gap is that I don't think he fully understands the structural barriers I'm running into — the S&OP framing issue, the fact that my KPIs measure capability but not impact. I surface these things, but in a 45-minute meeting that covers three topics, they don't get the depth of discussion they probably need. I haven't figured out whether I need a dedicated conversation specifically about those systemic issues, or whether I need to reframe them in terms that connect more naturally to how Michael thinks about supply chain performance. Probably both.
When you say you need "air cover" from Michael — give me a specific example of the last time you needed it and what he actually did.

Good question — and I want to be honest that the example I have is more modest than the phrase "air cover" might suggest.

About four months ago I was trying to get access to supplier financial data that procurement holds internally — payment terms history, invoice patterns, things that would significantly improve my supplier risk model. Jennifer's team was resistant. Not hostile, but slow-walking it — concerns about data governance, about how the data would be used, about whether sharing it created any contractual complications with suppliers.

I raised it with Michael and asked him to weigh in. He did — he sent an email to Jennifer saying the analytics team should have access to that data and asking her to work with me to sort out the governance questions. That unlocked the conversation. Jennifer's team engaged more seriously after that, and we eventually got access to most of what I needed, though it took another six weeks.

So Michael did act, and it worked. But I'd characterize what he did as more of a gentle nudge than genuine air cover. It was a collegial email, not a directive. And the six-week timeline tells you that even with his backing, the organizational friction was real.

What I haven't needed yet — but worry about — is a situation where I need him to make a more forceful intervention, like telling a functional leader that they need to actually implement an analytics recommendation rather than just consider it. I don't know how he'd respond to that ask, and I haven't tested it.
What recurring meetings do you attend beyond S&OP, and what decisions or outputs actually come out of each?

So beyond S&OP I have a few recurring touchpoints.

I run a **monthly analytics review** with supply chain leadership — that's my meeting, I own the agenda. I present KPI updates, model performance, new insights across functions. The honest output of that meeting is awareness, not decisions. People leave informed, occasionally ask follow-up questions, but I can't point to a specific decision that meeting has driven. It's more of a visibility exercise at this point.

I have **standing check-ins with individual function leads** — roughly monthly with David's planning team and Jennifer's procurement team, less regularly with logistics. Those are more tactical, project-specific. The outputs are more concrete there — a specific model getting refined, a data request getting scoped. That's where actual working-level collaboration happens.

I attend **some of Michael's strategy meetings** when there's a relevant analytics angle — but that's ad hoc, not recurring. When I'm in those I'm usually presenting a specific analysis rather than being a standing participant in the strategic conversation.

And I'm in various **project-level meetings** depending on what's active — right now that includes a network optimization workstream and a supplier risk pilot with two of Jennifer's category managers.

The pattern I notice is that my most structured recurring forum, the analytics review, produces the least tangible output. And the meetings where real decisions happen — S&OP, Michael's strategy sessions — I'm either embedded in someone else's agenda or attending episodically. That asymmetry bothers me. I have visibility but not consistent decision influence.
In the network optimization workstream you mentioned — who owns that decision, what specifically is being decided, and where does it stand right now?

So that workstream is looking at distribution center positioning and flow optimization in the midwest region — specifically whether we can reduce transit costs and lead times by adjusting which DCs serve which dealer clusters. It's a meaningful decision, probably $15-20 million in potential annual savings if the analysis supports a reconfiguration.

Ownership is somewhat ambiguous, which is itself a problem. Michael has framing authority over the strategic question. The logistics director, Robert, owns DC operations and would be responsible for any implementation. Finance has to validate the business case. So it's a shared decision with no single clear owner, which in my experience means decisions move slowly and the default is inertia.

My role is building the optimization model — I'm running network flow algorithms against our shipment data, dealer locations, DC costs. I'm essentially generating the analytical case for what the optimal configuration looks like.

Where it stands — I've completed an initial analysis that shows two scenarios worth serious consideration. I presented those to Robert about three weeks ago. His response was genuinely engaged but cautious. His concern is that the model doesn't fully capture operational complexity at the DC level — staffing constraints, lease terms, union considerations. Some of that is legitimate and I'm working to incorporate it. Some of it feels like it could become an endless list of reasons why the model isn't quite ready.

I'm trying to get a meeting with Michael, Robert, and Finance together to align on decision criteria before I refine the model further. Because right now I'm at risk of optimizing indefinitely without anyone committing to actually decide something.
Has that joint meeting with Michael, Robert, and Finance been scheduled yet, and if not, what's blocking it?

It hasn't been scheduled yet. I've sent one email proposing it, about two weeks ago, and haven't gotten traction.

The honest answer on what's blocking it is — I'm not entirely sure, and that uncertainty is itself informative. Robert responded to my email and said it made sense but that he wanted to review my updated model incorporating his operational constraints before bringing Finance in. Which is a reasonable position on its face. But it also means the sequencing is now: I update the model, Robert reviews it, then we schedule the joint meeting. That could be another six to eight weeks realistically.

Michael wasn't on that email thread. That was probably a mistake on my part — I went directly to Robert to schedule it without looping Michael in, partly because I didn't want to appear like I was escalating prematurely. But the effect is that there's no senior-level pressure on the timeline.

So what's blocking it is a combination of things. Robert has a legitimate sequencing concern. I made a tactical error by not involving Michael in the scheduling conversation. And there's no burning platform — nobody is feeling urgency about this decision right now, which means it'll keep getting deprioritized against operational issues that feel more immediate.

I think I need to bring this to my next one-on-one with Michael and ask him to help set a decision timeline. Not frame it as an escalation against Robert, but as a request to establish some forcing function so the workstream actually reaches a conclusion rather than drifting.
What are the 5 to 7 KPIs you're held accountable to, and what is the specific target for each?

My core KPIs are four areas, and I'll be transparent about where targets are firm versus directional.

**Engineering cost per vehicle** — we have a value engineering target tied to each program, typically a 2-3% cost reduction versus prior generation on the engineering-driven components. That's a hard target negotiated with the CPO.

**Vehicle quality metrics** — specifically warranty cost per unit in service and initial quality defect rates. We target below industry average on J.D. Power IQS and a warranty cost reduction of roughly 5% year-over-year. These flow directly from HQ commitments.

**Program schedule attainment** — hitting our engineering gates on time: design freeze, prototype completion, validation sign-off, Job 1. We track gate attainment as a percentage; we target 90% or better on-time gate completion across active programs.

**Engineering change impact** — this one's more internal. We track late engineering changes — meaning changes after design freeze — their frequency, cost impact, and downstream schedule impact. Honestly, the target here is directional: minimize unnecessary churn. There's no hard number that says "zero late changes" because sometimes changes are required. Safety findings, regulatory updates, test failures — those have to happen regardless of timing.

I'll be candid — my KPIs are primarily technical and program-focused. They're not formally linked to supply chain metrics like supplier lead times or expedite costs. That's an area where Lisa and I have had some honest conversations about whether the measurement framework should be tighter. It probably should.
What is the specific target for late engineering changes — even if directional — and who tracks and reports that number?

Honestly, that's where I have to be transparent — we don't have a crisp numerical target on late engineering changes the way we do on, say, warranty cost or schedule attainment.

My team tracks the number of engineering change notices issued after design freeze, categorized by type — safety-driven, regulatory, test failure, cost engineering, market feedback. We review that in our monthly program management meetings. I own that reporting internally, and it rolls up to the CPO in program reviews.

The categorization matters to me because not all late changes are equal. A change driven by a brake test failure is non-negotiable. A change driven by a cost engineering opportunity that could have been identified earlier — that's the one I want to reduce.

What I don't have is a hard target like "no more than X late changes per program." And I'll acknowledge that's probably a gap. Lisa has pushed on this, and her point is fair — from supply chain's perspective, the category distinction doesn't change the disruption impact. A late change is a late change regardless of why it happened. That's a tension I don't have a fully satisfying answer to yet.
Who is Lisa, and what is her role?

Lisa Morrison — she's our VP of Supply Chain Operations for North America. She owns supplier relationships, procurement, logistics, production scheduling from a supply chain perspective.

We interact regularly, particularly when engineering changes create downstream disruption for her team. She's the one who feels the impact most directly when we issue a late engineering change — her suppliers have to react, lead times get compressed, sometimes there are expedite costs.

Like I said, the relationship is respectful. We're aligned on the end goal — getting vehicles to production on time and at cost. We just sometimes have different views on how much flexibility engineering should have to optimize designs late in a program cycle.

Tell me about a specific late engineering change in the past 6 months that created conflict or significant disruption with Lisa's team — what was the change, when did it happen relative to design freeze, and what was the downstream impact?

There's one that comes to mind readily — it involved a thermal management component on one of our crossover programs. We were in validation testing and our thermal cycling results on the battery management system came back worse than our models predicted. This was roughly eight weeks after design freeze.

The engineering fix required a specification change on a component that was already in supplier tooling. Not a complete redesign, but enough that the supplier had to retool and requalify. Lisa's team had to manage that supplier relationship, compress the lead time, and I believe there were expedite costs involved. I don't own those numbers directly but my understanding is it was meaningful.

The conflict with Lisa was real. Her position was that our thermal modeling should have caught this earlier and that we moved to design freeze too quickly. My position was that physical testing sometimes reveals things simulation doesn't, and waiting longer before design freeze would have pushed Job 1. Neither of us was entirely wrong.

What made it harder was the timing — her team had already placed orders based on the original specification. So it wasn't just a forward-looking supplier adjustment, it was managing commitments that were already in motion.

That one led to a direct conversation between Lisa, myself, and the program manager about whether our simulation validation gates before design freeze were rigorous enough. We made some adjustments to our pre-freeze thermal validation protocol as a result. So something constructive came out of it, but the disruption was real.
Who is the program manager you mentioned, and does that person have authority to delay a design freeze if engineering validation data is insufficient?

The program manager on that specific crossover program is David Park. He reports into our Vehicle Program Management office, which sits under the CPO alongside my function.

On your second question — technically yes, David has authority to delay a design freeze. Program managers own the program schedule and the gate decisions. But in practice it's more complicated than that. Design freeze decisions involve engineering readiness, supplier commitments, production scheduling, and market timing. David can call a delay but he's doing that against significant pressure from multiple directions — supply chain has suppliers ready to move, manufacturing has production dates locked, sales and marketing has launch commitments.

So the honest answer is: the authority exists on paper, but exercising it has real consequences that create pressure against using it. In the thermal management situation, we made a judgment call that our simulation data was sufficient to proceed to freeze, and physical testing later told us otherwise. David could have pushed for more validation time before freeze, and in hindsight that probably would have been the right call.

That's actually part of what we discussed in the post-incident conversation — whether our gate criteria for design freeze are specific enough that a program manager has clear grounds to delay versus relying on judgment calls under schedule pressure. Right now it's more judgment-dependent than it should be, and that creates inconsistency.
What specific changes, if any, came out of that post-incident conversation — and have they been formally documented or are they still informal?

Honestly, the changes are partially documented but not as formally as they should be.

What we agreed to was strengthening the thermal validation requirements before design freeze — specifically, requiring physical prototype test data on thermal cycling, not just simulation sign-off, before a program can gate through to freeze. That's been captured in an internal engineering memo and communicated to my validation team leads.

What hasn't happened yet is updating the formal gate criteria documentation — the official program management gate checklist that David and program managers use. That's a gap. The intent is there, the practice is changing on active programs, but the formal process documentation hasn't been updated. That's something I need to close out.

The other piece that came out of the conversation was a commitment to earlier supply chain notification when validation data is showing risk — essentially flagging Lisa's team sooner if we're seeing test results that might require a spec change, rather than waiting until we have a confirmed finding. That one is genuinely informal right now. It's more of a personal commitment I made to Lisa than a structured process. Whether it actually happens consistently depends on my team leads recognizing those situations early enough, and I can't say with confidence that's happening reliably across all programs.

So the candid summary — directionally positive outcome, partially implemented, not fully institutionalized. That's probably not a satisfying answer but it's the accurate one.

When you said "earlier supply chain notification when validation data is showing risk" — what does that actually look like in practice today, who specifically on your team is responsible for making that call, and have they done it on any active program since that conversation?

That's a fair challenge and I have to be honest — in practice today it's not well-defined.

There's no formal trigger, no specific threshold that says "when test results show X deviation, notify supply chain within Y timeframe." It's more of an expectation I've communicated to my validation leads — if you're seeing results trending in a concerning direction, flag it up and we'll assess whether supply chain needs to know. The responsibility nominally sits with the validation lead on each program, but there's no named owner with a clear accountability structure around it.

Have they done it on any active program since that conversation? I'm aware of one instance where a validation lead flagged a potential issue to me and we had an internal discussion about it. Whether that resulted in a formal notification to Lisa's team — I'd have to check. I don't believe it did, because the issue resolved itself before we had a confirmed finding requiring a spec change. But I can't tell you with confidence that the loop was closed with supply chain even informally.

The honest assessment is that what I described to you as a commitment is closer to an intention that hasn't been operationalized. The conversation with Lisa was genuine and the intent was real, but I haven't put the structural elements in place — clear trigger criteria, named responsibility, documented notification protocol — that would make it reliable rather than dependent on individual judgment in the moment. That's a gap I'm acknowledging directly to you that I probably need to close more deliberately.
Who on your team are the validation leads by name, and how many active programs are they currently covering?

I can give you the names of my senior validation leads, though I want to be straightforward that program assignments shift as programs move through phases.

My two primary validation leads are Kevin Okafor, who handles powertrain and thermal systems validation, and Sarah Lindqvist, who covers chassis, safety systems, and electrical. There's a third lead, James Torrealba, who joined us about eight months ago and is handling some of the electrical architecture validation on our newer programs.

In terms of active program coverage — Kevin is currently across two programs in active validation phases. Sarah is covering three, which is honestly heavier than ideal. James is on two programs but with closer oversight from Kevin given his tenure.

The workload on Sarah is something I'm aware of. Three programs in concurrent validation creates real bandwidth pressure, and that's relevant to your earlier question — if I'm relying on individual judgment calls to decide when to notify supply chain of emerging risks, and my validation leads are stretched, the likelihood of those calls happening consistently and early enough goes down. That's a structural vulnerability I'm acknowledging.

I don't have a clean answer for it right now. We have a headcount request in for an additional validation engineer but that's not resolved yet. In the meantime Sarah's managing it, but I'd be misleading you if I said the workload isn't a risk factor for the kind of early warning commitment I described to Lisa.
What are your recurring meetings, how often do they occur, and what decisions or outputs come out of each?

Let me walk through the main ones.

**Vehicle program management reviews** — monthly, with program managers including David Park. We review gate status, schedule attainment, open engineering issues, and late change log. Decisions coming out of those are gate approvals or holds, resource allocation to critical path items, and escalations to the CPO when programs are at risk.

**Engineering design reviews** — these are program-specific, not on a fixed calendar. They happen at key development milestones. Outputs are design approval or return for revision, open action items with owners and due dates. These are where the substantive technical decisions get made.

**Design-for-manufacturability sessions** — these are periodic, roughly quarterly, with manufacturing and supply chain participating. Lisa's team is in the room for these. Outputs are supposed to be design adjustments that improve manufacturability or reduce cost without compromising performance. Honestly the quality of these varies by program.

**My staff meeting with validation and program leads** — weekly. That's where Kevin, Sarah, and James report out on test status, emerging issues, resource constraints. Decisions are internal prioritization and escalation calls.

**Crisis or issue-specific meetings** — these happen reactively when something breaks. The thermal management situation generated several of those.

What I'd note is that I don't have a recurring structured touchpoint specifically with Lisa outside of the monthly program reviews and the DFM sessions. Our interaction is either scheduled-formal or crisis-reactive. There's not much in between, and that's probably relevant to the early warning conversation we were just having.

In the design-for-manufacturability sessions — who specifically from Lisa's team attends, what decisions have actually come out of one in the past six months, and when you said the quality varies, what does a low-quality session look like versus a high-quality one?

On attendance from Lisa's team — it's typically one of her senior procurement managers, usually a person named Rachel Huang who covers the component categories most relevant to whatever program we're reviewing. Occasionally Lisa attends herself but that's not the norm. My honest read is that Lisa's team views these sessions as lower priority than their day-to-day supplier management work, which affects the level of engagement.

On actual decisions from the past six months — I'd point to one session on our compact SUV program where we identified a bracket redesign that simplified assembly and eliminated a supplier-specific tooling requirement. That was a genuine win — cost reduction and improved manufacturability, identified early enough to execute cleanly. That's the kind of output these sessions are supposed to generate.

But I'd struggle to give you a second concrete example from the past six months that resulted in a documented decision with clear follow-through. That's telling.

On the quality variation question — a high-quality session has the right people in the room with authority to make decisions, engineering has specific design questions we need manufacturing or supply chain input on, and we leave with documented action items and owners. A low-quality session is essentially a presentation — engineering shows designs, attendees ask some questions, nothing gets decided, follow-up is vague. Those sessions happen because they're on the calendar, not because there's a specific problem to solve. And those are the majority, if I'm being honest with myself.
